original_hypothesis.title: Duration + timestamp cast to uint64 can overflow in far-future timestamps, potentially bypassing lock-time validation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: selfStake/extendSelfStake/communityStake/extendCommunityStake compute unlockTime = duration + uint64(block.timestamp). If block.timestamp exceeds uint64 max (or if duration addition overflows uint64), unlockTime wraps, potentially passing the 12-104 week window checks incorrectly and allowing malformed unlock times.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Not practically exploitable on present-day chains (uint64 timestamp overflow is extremely far away), but it is a concrete code correctness defect (unsafe narrowing cast + unchecked addition). It can also become relevant if the code is reused in non-EVM environments or if timestamp semantics differ.

proof_of_signal.lines: selfStake approx. 116-132; extendSelfStake approx. 149-170; communityStake approx. 198-220; extendCommunityStake approx. 238-270
proof_of_signal.pattern: uint64 unlockTime = duration + uint64(block.timestamp); (unchecked uint64 arithmetic + cast)

preconditions: block.timestamp near or above 2^64-1, or duration crafted to overflow uint64 when added to uint64(block.timestamp)
Solidity does not revert on uint64 overflow (it does revert on overflow, but only after the cast; the cast itself truncates if block.timestamp > uint64 max in future environments)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: narrowing cast of timestamp to uint64 + addition
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: wrap/truncation could lead to incorrect unlockTime and lock checks
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No use of uint256 for time math, no explicit bounds on duration beyond derived unlockTime comparisons

analysis_questions: Does Solidity 0.8.23 insert checks for the uint64 cast (it does not; it truncates) and for uint64 addition (it reverts on overflow)?
Could a truncated uint64(block.timestamp) ever occur on target chains (practically no, but check for nonstandard timestamp semantics)?
Should the contract standardize on uint256 time computations and store uint64 only after bounds checks?
validation_requirements: Confirm compiled behavior for uint64(block.timestamp) cast
Add tests simulating large timestamps in a forked/custom VM if possible
Recommend using uint256 arithmetic then casting after checks

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: In an environment where block.timestamp exceeds uint64, the cast truncates, potentially producing an unlockTime that passes checks but is not actually a valid future time, undermining lock enforcement.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Could undermine mandatory lock period in extreme/future conditions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Potentially all stakers if time math becomes incorrect
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Upgradeable fix: use uint256 for computations and store validated uint64

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely no tests for type casting/time overflow edge cases

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor for abnormal unlockTime values if ever deployed long-term

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
